title: Barrett's The Magus revives ceremonial magic
blurb: Francis Barrett's compendium recycled Agrippa for a new Romantic audience, quietly reopening the door to ceremonial magic that the Enlightenment had shut.
source: Ronald Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon

title: Walter Scott popularises 'glamour'
blurb: Scott's Letters on Demonology carried the Scots word glamour — a magic spell that deceives the eye — into standard English, whence it drifted toward dazzle and fashion.

title: Spiritualism begins with the Fox sisters
blurb: Rapping 'spirit' communications in upstate New York launched Spiritualism, a mass movement of mediums, séances, and a new industry of talking with the dead.
source: Ronald Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon

title: Éliphas Lévi codifies modern occultism
blurb: Lévi's Dogme et Rituel de la Haute Magie fused Kabbalah, Tarot, and ceremonial magic into the synthesis that every later occult order would inherit — and coined much of its imagery.
source: Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon

title: Blavatsky founds the Theosophical Society
blurb: H. P. Blavatsky's Theosophy wove Western esotericism together with Indian and Tibetan ideas, globalising the occult and giving 'esoteric' its modern currency.
source: TheosophicalAlchemyDB; Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon

title: The Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn
blurb: The Golden Dawn systematised Western ritual magic into grades and correspondences, training a generation — Yeats, Machen, Crowley — and shaping nearly all magic that followed.
mtg: The immediate ancestor of pop-culture 'ceremonial magic' aesthetics.
source: Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon

title: Frazer's The Golden Bough
blurb: James Frazer's comparative study framed magic as humanity's first, failed science of sympathy and contagion — a framework that shaped a century of thinking about ritual.
source: J. G. Frazer, The Golden Bough

title: Mathers translates the Key of Solomon
blurb: S. L. MacGregor Mathers put the Solomonic pentacles and, later, the Goetia into accessible English — the texts that carried demon-and-seal magic into the twentieth century.

title: Crowley and the birth of 'Magick'
blurb: Aleister Crowley received The Book of the Law and spelled magic 'magick' to mark the real art from stage tricks, becoming the twentieth century's most notorious magus.
source: Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon

title: The Rider–Waite–Smith Tarot
blurb: Pamela Colman Smith's fully-illustrated deck made every card a readable scene, becoming the template for modern Tarot divination and its imagery.
mtg: The illustrated card as an instrument of meaning — a distant cousin of the MTG card.
source: Michael Dummett, The Game of Tarot (context)

title: Murray's witch-cult hypothesis
blurb: Margaret Murray argued the witch trials had persecuted a real pagan religion — a thesis scholars later demolished, but which directly inspired the invention of Wicca.
source: Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon

title: Lovecraft weaponises 'eldritch'
blurb: H. P. Lovecraft took a dusty Scots word for 'otherworldly' and made eldritch the defining adjective of cosmic horror — the uncanny beyond human categories.
mtg: The horror of the Eldrazi and every 'eldritch' card runs through Lovecraft.

title: Jung's Psychology and Alchemy
blurb: C. G. Jung reread alchemy as a projection of the psyche's individuation — nigredo to rubedo as stages of the soul — reviving the imagery for the twentieth century.
source: Jung, Psychology and Alchemy (alchemy corpus); Lyndy Abraham

title: Graves' The White Goddess
blurb: Robert Graves' poetic 'grammar of myth' gave modern paganism its lunar Triple Goddess, shaping the theology of the Wicca soon to be born.
source: Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon

title: Gardner reveals Wicca
blurb: Gerald Gardner's Witchcraft Today announced a surviving witch-religion — actually a new one, stitched from Murray, Crowley, and folklore — launching modern Wicca and its besom, cauldron, and athame.
source: Ronald Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon

title: Tolkien fixes the modern wizard
blurb: The Lord of the Rings gave fantasy its archetypal wizard in Gandalf — staff, robe, and hidden angelic nature — the mould nearly every later game wizard was cast in.
mtg: The template behind MTG's 1,288 Wizard creatures.

title: Yates' Giordano Bruno and the Hermetic Tradition
blurb: Frances Yates argued that Renaissance Hermetic magic was a driver of the Scientific Revolution — the book that made the serious academic study of magic respectable.
mtg: The scholarship this whole project stands on.
source: Yates, Giordano Bruno and the Hermetic Tradition (renaissance magic corpus)
